sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after pressing DOWN. Let me check what changed by comparing the before and after frames. The green bar at the top seems to have changed slightly (the right end looks different). Let me check the segmentation to see what moved.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after DOWN
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("After DOWN:")
for n in nodes:
    print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}...")

# Compare with previous frame
if last_transition:
    before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    after_seg = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
    before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before_seg['nodes']}
    after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after_seg['nodes']}
    
    print("\nChanges:")
    for nid in after_nodes:
        if nid in before_nodes:
            bn = before_nodes[nid]
            an = after_nodes[nid]
            if bn['boundary'] != an['boundary'] or bn['pixels'] != an['pixels']:
                print(f"  Node {nid} ({an['color']}): boundary changed")
                print(f"    Before: {bn['boundary']}")
                print(f"    After:  {an['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"  Node {nid} ({after_nodes[nid]['color']}): NEW")
    for nid in before_nodes:
        if nid not in after_nodes:
            print(f"  Node {nid} ({before_nodes[nid]['color']}): GONE")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The node IDs got shuffled, but I can track by color. Let me look at what actually moved:
- The green (N) bar shrank from 64 pixels to 62 pixels (cols 0-61 instead of 0-63). A white (W) 2-pixel piece appeared at cols 62-63.
- The blue (b) bar moved DOWN: from rows 16-19 to rows 20-23 (moved 4 rows down).
- The charcoal (c) block stayed at rows 1-3, cols 36-39 (same position).
- The magenta (M) block stayed at rows 4-7, cols 36-39 (same position).
- The yellow (Y) blocks stayed at same positions.
- The gray (w) bar stayed at rows 60-63.
So pressing DOWN made the blue bar move down by 4 rows! The green timer also shrank by 2 pixels. The charcoal and magenta blocks didn't move, so they're not the player.
The blue bar is the controllable object. Let me verify this by trying other directions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The blue bar moved DOWN by 4 rows! It went from rows 16-19 to rows 20-23.
# The green timer bar shrank by 2 pixels.
# Charcoal and magenta blocks stayed put - they're not the player.
# The blue bar is the controllable entity.

# Let me try LEFT to see if the blue bar moves left
predict(expect_change=True, note='Pressing LEFT - blue bar should move left')
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
